Q1: Yes, our three categories map cleanly onto True/False/Unknown — we treat entailment as True, contradiction as False, and neutral as Unknown. We don't need to distinguish between different flavors of 'unknown' (e.g., insufficient info vs. open-world); a single neutral bucket is sufficient for our accuracy metric.
Q2: We're primarily interested in broad coverage of classical deductive FOL — quantifiers, negation, conditionals, and multi-step chains. Temporal, modal, arithmetic, and probabilistic reasoning are out of scope for this particular evaluation; we may build separate suites for those later.
Q3: We're presenting premises and conclusions in formal logical notation, but realistically the models will also benefit from any accompanying natural language. We haven't fully committed — we're considering both pure-symbolic and hybrid (NL + FOL) presentations to see how the model handles each, since real reasoning use cases often involve both.
